channel.title: Science - Latest - Google News
channel.copyright: Copyright © 2026 Google. All rights reserved. This XML feed is made available solely for the purpose of rendering Google News results within a personal feed reader for personal, non-commercial use. Any other use of the feed is expressly prohibited. By accessing this feed or using these results in any manner whatsoever, you agree to be bound by the foregoing restrictions.

item.title: T rex fossil ‘Gus’ sells for $50.1m at New York auction, setting new record - The Guardian
item.description:
1. T rex fossil ‘Gus’ sells for $50.1m at New York auction, setting new record  The Guardian
  2. Rare T. rex fossil sells for record $50.1 million at auction  CNN
  3. Sale of multimillion-dollar T rex skeleton is big headache for scientists  The Guardian
  4. Dinosaurs are selling for millions. Do collectors know what they’re buying?  Financial Times
  5. Sotheby’s Big T. Rex Auction Raises Concerns Hype and Wealth Are Upending Science  WIRED

item.description: Scientists show that DNA can last for up to 50,000 years in Africa — much longer than previously thought  Live Science

  4. Astronomers Discover Four New White Dwarfs in Solar Neighborhood  Sci.News

1. Honeycomb structures spotted on Mars photo of the day for July 14, 2026  Space
  2. Curiosity discovers strange honeycomb structures on Mars  Universe Space Tech
  3. Honeycomb structures spotted on Mars | Space photo of the day for July 14, 2026  MSN

item.title: Beneath Europa’s fractured ice may be an ocean containing more liquid water than all of Earth’s oceans combined, kept warm in complete darkness by the gravitational pull of Jupiter - Space Daily
item.description:
1. Beneath Europa’s fractured ice may be an ocean containing more liquid water than all of Earth’s oceans combined, kept warm in complete darkness by the gravitational pull of Jupiter  Space Daily
  2. Scientists searching for alien life are looking ever further out, to Europa, a moon of Jupiter hiding an ocean beneath its ice that could resemble the dark seas where life survives on Earth  Space Daily
  3. A 2026 study found that Jupiter's ocean moon Europa may have a geologically quiet seafloor with little active faulting — meaning the vast hidden ocean long treated as our best bet for alien life may lack the very energy that life would need to survive  Space Daily
  4. Europa, one of Jupiter’s moons, is sealed beneath a shell of ice, but the ocean hidden below may contain twice as much liquid water as every ocean on Earth combined — and Jupiter’s gravity may have kept it liquid for billions of years  Space Daily

item.title: You can see this comet tonight. But you'll need a telescope or binoculars - USA Today
item.description:
1. You can see this comet tonight. But you'll need a telescope or binoculars  USA Today
  2. Don’t Miss a Comet Sighting and a Great View of the Milky Way This Month—Here’s When to Look Up  marthastewart.com
  3. Comet Tempel 2 amazing dust trail  Sky & Telescope
  4. How You Could See a Comet This Week  Time Magazine
  5. Comet Tempel 2, the size of the one that wiped out the dinosaurs, is heading toward Earth this summer  Diario AS

item.title: Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman - The New York Times
item.description:
1. Researchers Claim to Have Solved the Perplexing ‘Reverse Sprinkler’ Problem That Stumped Feynman  The New York Times
  2. Solution to Feynman’s reverse sprinkler puzzle also applies to “silly sprinklers”  Ars Technica
  3. 'Silly sprinklers' put in reverse to further unravel decades-old physics puzzle  Phys.org
  4. NYU Researchers Identify Physical Mechanisms Behind Reverse Sprinkler Motion  geneonline.com
  5. Feynman’s Famous Physics Problem of a Sprinkler Running in Reverse has a Surprising New Solution  The Debrief

item.title: A large amount of the Universe is missing. Scientists think they may have found it - BBC Sky at Night Magazine
item.description:
1. A large amount of the Universe is missing. Scientists think they may have found it  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  2. Vera Rubin watched stars at the edges of galaxies moving far faster than visible matter could explain and found one of the clearest early signs that most of the universe is made of something we cannot see  Space Daily
  3. Strangely familiar: Is dark matter normal stuff in disguise?  New Scientist
  4. If dark matter is everywhere, why do some galaxies seem to contain none at all?  BBC Sky at Night Magazine
  5. Most of every galaxy is made of matter nobody has ever seen, detected, or explained — and the clearest early proof came from an astronomer named Vera Rubin, who simply measured how fast the outer stars of galaxies were spinning  Space Daily

item.title: Your Dominant Hand Isn't Actually Hard-Wired, New Study Suggests - ScienceAlert
item.description:
1. Your Dominant Hand Isn't Actually Hard-Wired, New Study Suggests  ScienceAlert
  2. Your dominant hand may not be born-in: A study that taped people’s elbows shut found both sides quickly learned to write, hinting practice is what makes one hand better  The Times of India
  3. Why is one hand better than the other? Study has the answer  Tech Explorist
  4. Being able to write easily with your dominant hand may not be something you're born with, but rather the result of 'lifelong practice.'  GIGAZINE
  5. Dominant hand excels through practice rather than brain advantage  News-Medical

item.title: Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future - Gizmodo
item.description:
1. Scientists Finally Solved the Mystery of Earth’s ‘Great Dying.’ It’s Bad News for Our Future  Gizmodo
  2. Around 252 million years ago, volcanoes across what is now Siberia erupted repeatedly for more than a million years, releasing perhaps 100,000 billion tonnes of carbon dioxide and helping wipe out roughly 90 per cent of marine species in the deadliest mas  Space Daily
  3. Scientists may have solved the 252 million-year-old mystery behind Earth's greatest mass extinction  The Times of India
  4. Metabolic vulnerability confirmed as cause of Earth’s biggest mass extinction  Open Access Government
  5. The Permian Analogy: Climate Literacy and the Urgency We Cannot Grasp  Countercurrents

item.title: The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces - Space Daily
item.description:
1. The James Webb Space Telescope found tiny “little red dots” that looked like impossibly compact galaxies — but astronomers now suspect many may be “black hole stars,” young black holes wrapped in gas so dense that the material falling into them produces  Space Daily
  2. A mysterious “red dot” spotted in space: are we witnessing a cosmic birth?  Futura, le média qui explore le monde
  3. How the Webb Telescope is Influencing Astrophysicists’ Theories  Adafruit
  4. Backyard Black Holes  Astrobites
  5. Black holes buried in mysterious 'little red dot' galaxies could blast cosmic ghosts at Earth  Space

item.description:

  4. Scientists solve mystery of how seals hear in air and underwater  Oceanographic Magazine

  4. Scientists just confirmed Einstein’s greatest theory—again  Scientific American
  5. An orbiting disco ball gave Einstein’s theory its most precise test yet  Ars Technica

item.title: China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft chased Earth’s tiny quasi-moon for 400 days and 1 billion kilometres — but just as it returned the first close-up image, new evidence challenged the leading theory that Kamoʻoalewa is a fragment blasted from the Moon - Space Daily
item.description:
1. China’s Tianwen-2 spacecraft chased Earth’s tiny quasi-moon for 400 days and 1 billion kilometres — but just as it returned the first close-up image, new evidence challenged the leading theory that Kamoʻoalewa is a fragment blasted from the Moon  Space Daily
  2. In Deep Space, China Meets Earth’s ‘Amazing’ Quasi-Moon  The New York Times
  3. Chinese Spacecraft Approaches Mysterious Object Near Earth  Futurism
  4. Tianwen-2 arrives at asteroid Kamo’oalewa, first image revealed  SpaceNews
  5. After a Billion Kilometres, China's Asteroid Hunter Finally Arrives  Universe Today

item.title: Physicists confirm 20-year-old theory that could boost quantum technology - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Physicists confirm 20-year-old theory that could boost quantum technology  Phys.org
  2. Entanglement Goes Steady  American Physical Society
  3. Quantum Machines’ Parametric Systems Generate High-Throughput Entanglement  Quantum Zeitgeist
  4. Quantum Networks Benefit From Stabilized, “On Demand” Entanglement  Quantum Zeitgeist
  5. UIUC And ISTA Demonstrate Steady Entanglement Of Qubits  Quantum Zeitgeist

item.title: 'Vanishingly Rare' Discovery: T. Rex Hatchlings Were Smaller Than a Cat And Born by The Dozen - ScienceAlert
item.description: 'Vanishingly Rare' Discovery: T. Rex Hatchlings Were Smaller Than a Cat And Born by The Dozen  ScienceAlert

item.title: In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w - Space Daily
item.description:
1. In 2023, seismologists at the Australian National University, reading the echoes of earthquakes ringing back and forth through the whole planet, confirmed Earth hides an innermost core inside its inner core, a distinct iron ball roughly 1,300 kilometres across w  Space Daily
  2. Academia Sinica - Research Findings | Earth’s mantle locally heats up our planet’s core  sinica.edu.tw
  3. Earth’s inner core is an iron-rich ball as hot as the surface of the Sun, kept solid only because the crushing pressure of the entire planet raises the temperature at which iron can melt  Space Daily
  4. The Earth's inner core grows by roughly a millimetre a year as molten iron freezes onto it, and that slow crystallisation releases the energy that stirs the outer core and keeps the planet's magnetic field alive  Space Daily
  5. Earth’s inner core is not rock but a sphere of solid iron-rich metal, smaller than the Moon yet probably more massive. As the planet gradually loses heat, liquid metal freezes onto its surface, expanding it by roughly a millimetre each year.  Space Daily

item.title: New 3D thermal cloak hides objects from heat in any direction - Phys.org
item.description:
1. New 3D thermal cloak hides objects from heat in any direction  Phys.org
  2. New 3D-printed ‘invisibility’ cloak can hide objects from infrared cameras  Interesting Engineering
  3. First-ever 3D thermal cloak created by U of I team  WCIA.com
  4. Tech Tuesday: Scientists create heat cloaking device  WLNS 6 News
  5. 3D-printed thermal cloak makes objects invisible to infrared cameras  heise online

item.title: Astronomers uncover the earliest known signs of galaxy-building in action - Phys.org
item.description:
1. Astronomers uncover the earliest known signs of galaxy-building in action  Phys.org
  2. Not clumpy, chaotic but earliest galaxies were remarkably organised, discovery shows  The Indian Express
  3. The oldest nuclear disk changes our understanding of the evolution of galaxies  Universe Space Tech
  4. When did the first galaxies form? Earlier than we thought possible  New Scientist
  5. James Webb has caught at least five galaxies colliding about 800 million years after the Big Bang, flinging heavy elements out into space far earlier than any model predicted — evidence the early universe was already messy and mature when it should still ha  Space Daily

item.title: A satellite launched in 1958, no bigger than a grapefruit and silent since 1964, is still the oldest human-made object circling Earth — and it is expected to keep quietly orbiting overhead for another few centuries - Space Daily
item.description: A satellite launched in 1958, no bigger than a grapefruit and silent since 1964, is still the oldest human-made object circling Earth — and it is expected to keep quietly orbiting overhead for another few centuries  Space Daily

item.title: After Nearly 50 Years In The Depths Of Space, Voyager 1 Still Sends Messages That Take 23 Hours To Reach Earth - Yahoo
item.description:
1. After Nearly 50 Years In The Depths Of Space, Voyager 1 Still Sends Messages That Take 23 Hours To Reach Earth  Yahoo
  2. Voyager's signal now reaches Earth as a whisper measured in fractions of a billionth of a watt, fainter than almost anything our antennas were built to hear  Space Daily
  3. Voyager 1 Has Traveled Through Space at 38,000 Mph Since 1977 And Still Hasn't Reached 1 Light-Day  ScienceAlert
  4. Voyager 1 launched in 1977 with onboard computers holding less memory than a single photo on a modern phone — and that 1970s machine is still running, sending data back to Earth from interstellar space  ScienceBlog.com
  5. NASA's Voyager 1 Only Has Two Active Science Instruments Left After Latest System Shutdown  Hypebeast

item.description: Why Am I Left-Handed?  Quanta Magazine

item.title: A new paper argues that Earth's gravity is a hidden anchor for consciousness — and that losing it in orbit loosens the mind in a way that echoes psychedelics - Space Daily
item.description:
1. A new paper argues that Earth's gravity is a hidden anchor for consciousness — and that losing it in orbit loosens the mind in a way that echoes psychedelics  Space Daily
  2. What happens to your brain in space?  BBC
  3. Scientists Say Gravity Has Been Holding Your Consciousness Together  Popular Mechanics
  4. Astronauts returning from long missions can initially struggle to stand, walk, and judge the weight of ordinary objects — because the brain must relearn a gravitational world it once treated as automatic  Space Daily
  5. Space Travel and Brain Changes: Risks of Mars Missions  RaillyNews

item.title: We tend to assume Earth is a rare cosmic fluke, but a full analysis of NASA's Kepler data found at least 300 million rocky, habitable-zone planets around Sun-like stars in the Milky Way alone — and that is the conservative count - Space Daily

item.title: Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update - autoevolution
item.description:
1. Spacecraft Heading for Asteroid NASA Slapped so Hard It Changed Its Orbit Gets Crucial OTA Update  autoevolution
  2. A spacecraft called DART was deliberately crashed into an asteroid in 2022 to test whether humanity could deflect one, and it worked — the asteroid's orbit changed measurably, the first time humans have intentionally altered the movement of a natural body in  ScienceBlog.com
  3. NASA mission changed orbit of asteroid around Sun  The Grainger College of Engineering
  4. What Hera Will Find At The Asteroid DART Crashed Into  WorldAtlas

  4. Falling spacecraft debris increasingly surviving reentry, raising safety concerns  MSN
  5. Adapting Satellite Design to the Growing Space Debris Problem  AZoQuantum

item.title: NASA’s New Horizons travelled 3 billion miles over 9 years to reach Pluto – but it was moving too fast to - The Times of India
item.description:
1. NASA’s New Horizons travelled 3 billion miles over 9 years to reach Pluto – but it was moving too fast to  The Times of India
  2. On This Day: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  upi.com
  3. New Horizons flew past Pluto so quickly that an encounter nine years in the making was effectively over within hours — but the data gathered during that brief passage took another 15 months to trickle home  Space Daily
  4. Is an old NASA probe about to redraw the frontier of the solar system?  New Scientist
  5. On This Day, July 14: NASA's New Horizons gets closest look yet at Pluto  Yahoo

item.title: Are We Missing the Universe's "Noosignatures"? - Universe Today
item.description: Are We Missing the Universe's "Noosignatures"?  Universe Today

item.title: Astronomers have spent decades searching nearby stars for another Earth, and now they may have found one - The Times of India
item.description:
1. Astronomers have spent decades searching nearby stars for another Earth, and now they may have found one  The Times of India
  2. Astronomers have found a super-Earth just 25 light-years away — close enough by cosmic standards that it sits in the small category of worlds scientists consider genuinely worth searching for signs of life  Space Daily
  3. Scientists Say They’ve Identified an Earth-Like Planet Right Next Door  Futurism
  4. Alien life on nearby exoplanet more likely than previously thought  The Jerusalem Post
  5. Mass revision boosts habitability odds for nearby super-Earth  MSN
